{{< pagebreak >}}

# Day 6 — Repeat without expecting perfection {#day6}

```{=typst}
#wcg-lead[Today is about keeping your tools going through normal, imperfect days.]
#wcg-lesson-art("repeat", caption: [Steady, imperfect practice — one gap, and it continues])
```

## Why this matters

In a real-world study of habit formation, a new habit took about two months on
average to feel automatic — with a wide range from a few weeks to many months — and
missing a single day did not stop the habit from forming.^11^ There is no magic
number, and one skipped day is not a broken streak; it is just a day.

When a slip happens, how you respond matters. Kindness rather than harsh
self-criticism is linked with more motivation to try again.^12^ Being gentle with
yourself is not soft — it is practical.

## An everyday example

Say a busy week costs you a day or two. The old story says, "I failed, so why
bother." The kinder, truer story says, "That was a full week — I'll pick it up
tomorrow." The second story is the one that keeps habits alive.

```{=typst}
#wcg-action[
  Track your practice lightly — just "yes" or "not yet." Then plan for a day that
  might be hard, so a likely miss does not surprise you.
]
```

> **Notice.**
>
> Did I practice today — yes, or not yet?

{{< include assets/worksheets/ws-6-repetition-tracker.qmd >}}
